mechanism_narrative: Consistent growth in retail sales (RSAFS) indicates robust consumer demand, which directly translates to higher corporate revenues and earnings, thereby driving positive S&P 500 forward returns over a 20-day horizon.
evidence_refs: fact:Strong consumer spending supports economic growth
failure_condition: The 20-day forward returns of the S&P 500 show no statistically significant positive correlation with the month-over-month growth rate of RSAFS over any 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: A significant decline in the unemployment rate (UNRATE) below a critical threshold signals a tightening labor market, which typically precedes wage inflation and potential corporate margin compression, leading to negative equity returns over a 60-day horizon.
evidence_refs: fact:Tight labor markets can lead to inflation
failure_condition: S&P 500 60-day forward returns are positive in more than 50% of instances where UNRATE drops below 5.0% and remains there for at least one month.

mechanism_narrative: When inflation (CPIAUCSL) is rising significantly while the federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) remains relatively low, it indicates an accommodative monetary policy relative to price pressures, which typically supports equity valuations and drives positive S&P 500 returns over 20 days.
evidence_refs: fact:Low real rates support asset prices
failure_condition: S&P 500 20-day forward returns are negative or flat (within +/- 0.5%) in more than 50% of periods where CPIAUCSL growth is high and FEDFUNDS is low, as per the trigger.

mechanism_narrative: During periods of economic recovery, a strong rebound in housing starts (HOUST) indicates renewed consumer and business confidence, signaling future economic activity and driving short-term positive sentiment for equities over 5 trading days.
evidence_refs: fact:Housing is a leading indicator of economic health
failure_condition: S&P 500 5-day forward returns are not significantly positive (t-test p > 0.10) following HOUST surges in identified recovery regimes.

mechanism_narrative: A significant and sustained decline in industrial production (INDPRO) signals a contraction in manufacturing and overall supply-side economic activity, leading to lower corporate earnings expectations and negative equity returns over a 60-day horizon.
failure_condition: S&P 500 60-day forward returns are positive in more than 40% of instances where INDPRO shows a sustained decline as per the trigger condition.

mechanism_narrative: Consistent job growth, as measured by non-farm payrolls (PAYEMS), indicates a healthy and expanding labor market. This supports consumer spending and corporate revenues, which generally leads to positive equity performance over a 20-day horizon.
failure_condition: The correlation between PAYEMS month-over-month growth and S&P 500 20-day forward returns is consistently negative or statistically insignificant over any 5-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: When the Federal Funds Rate (FEDFUNDS) is rising rapidly while GDP growth (GDPC1) is simultaneously slowing, it indicates a restrictive monetary policy that is stifling economic growth, leading to negative equity returns over a 60-day horizon.
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS 3-month change > 0.5% AND GDPC1 2-quarter annualized growth < 2.0%
failure_condition: S&P 500 60-day forward returns are positive in more than 30% of periods where FEDFUNDS is rising and GDPC1 growth is low, as per the trigger conditions.

mechanism_narrative: A significant acceleration in GDP growth (GDPC1) signals strong underlying economic momentum and robust demand, which can generate immediate positive sentiment and short-term gains for the S&P 500 over a 5-day horizon.
evidence_refs: fact:Strong GDP growth boosts market confidence
failure_condition: S&P 500 5-day forward returns are not significantly positive (t-test p > 0.05) when GDPC1 growth exceeds 4.0% for two consecutive quarters.
